coredata.dc:description: Red fluorescent hydrogels possessing injectable and self-healing properties have widespread potential in biomedical field. It is still a challenge to achieve a biomacromolecules based dynamic hydrogels simultaneously combining with excellent red fluorescence, good mechanical properties, and biocompatibility. Here we first explore hydrophilic inclusion complex of (R-CDs@α-CD) derived from hydrophobic red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) and α-cyclodextrin (α-CD), and then achieved a red fluorescent and dynamic polysaccharide R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. The nanocomposite hydrogel can be fabricated through controlled doping of red fluorescent R-CDs@α-CD into dynamic polymer networks, taking reversibly crosslinked N-carboxyethyl chitosan (CEC) and oxidized sodium alginate (OSA) as an example. The versatile red fluorescent hydrogel simultaneously combines the features of injection, biocompatibility, and augmented mechanical properties and self-healing behavior, especially in rapid self-recovery even after integration. The R-CDs@α-CD uniformly dispersed into dynamic hydrogel played the role of killing two birds with one stone, that is, endowing red emission of a hydrophilic fluorescent substance, and improving mechanical and self-healing properties as a dynamic nano-crosslinker, via forming hydrogen bonds as reversible crosslinkings. The novel red fluorescent and dynamic hydrogel based on polysaccharides is promising for using as biomaterials in biomedical field.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of red emissive R-CDs and R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex. A) Schematic diagram of synthesis and separation of R-CDs (i), preparation of R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex (ii), TEM and HRTEM images, and size distribution of R-CDs (iii). B) UV spectra (i) photoluminescent (PL) emission and excitation (ii) spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@α-CD, and PL emission spectra of R-CDs@α-CD excited by different wavelengths of light (iii). C) FTIR, D) XRD patterns, and E) TGA of R-CDs@α-CD and contrastive samples.

Preparation and characterization of the dynamically photoluminescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. A) Facile fabrication of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel (i) involves mixing the R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex with CEC in PBS (ii) and adding OSA dissolved in PBS (iii). B) The gelation time of hydrogels loading various amounts of R-CD@α-CD. C) The FTIR spectrum of R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA and CEC-l-OSA hydrogel, D) SEM images and graphs of pore diameter distribution of the hydrogels (scale bar: 50 μm). E) The PL excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel (i), and the PL emission spectra of the hydrogels loading with various content of R-CDs@α-CD (ii). The data with error bar are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 5).

The compressive mechanical properties of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel loading with 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 mg/mL of R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex. A) Strain-stress profiles (i), stress and Young's modulus (ii) of the hydrogels. B) Successive loading-unloading curves (i), stress and energy dissipation (ii) at 10 %–50 % strain for 1 cycle. C) Successive loading-unloading curves (i), stress and energy dissipation (ii) of the R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel at 50 % strain for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 cycles. Stress and energy dissipation at 50 % for 1 cycle (D) and at 50 % for 50 cycles (E) of the hydrogels loading with various amounts of R-CDs@α-CD. Error bar represents standard deviations with n ≥ 5.

Injectability, self-healing, and shape-adaptability of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. A) Injectable and self-healing process of the hydrogel. B) 3D rotational microscopy images of self-healing of the incision between two pieces of hydrogel cut in half over time. C) Oscillatory frequency sweeps curves of the hydrogel loading with various amount of R-CDs@α-CD. D) Oscillatory strain sweep measurements of the R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. E) Rapid self-healing property of R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel demonstrated by continuous step strain (1 % strain→500 % strain→1 % strain) measurements at 37 °C. F) Schematic illustration of the proposed self-healing mechanism of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel.

Compressive mechanical properties of integrated R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel constituted by gel particles after injection and self-healing. A) Photographs of integrated hydrogel after injection and self-healing experienced compression test and recovery (scar bar: 1 cm). B) stress-strain profiles (i), stress and Young's modulus (ii) of the integrated hydrogel after injection and self-healing. B) Photographs of injected and self-healed hydrogel experienced compression test and recovery (scar bar: 1 cm). C) Successive loading-unloading curves (i), stress and energy dissipation (ii) at 10 %, 20 %, 30 %, 40 %, 50 % strain. D) Successive loading-unloading curves (i), stress and energy dissipation (ii) at 50 % strain for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 cycles. E) Stress and energy dissipation at 50 % for 1 cycle and F) self-healing efficiency of the hydrogel doped with various amounts of R-CDs@α-CD. The hydrogel samples self-healed for 2 h at 37 °C. Error bar represents standard deviations with n ≥ 5.

Cytocompatibility and haemocompatibility of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels. A) Live/Dead staining micrographs of HUVECs cultured by the extract of R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel at different culture times comparing with control and CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. B) Cell viability of HUVECs cultured in the extracts of hydrogels loading with various amount of inclusion complex. C) Cell proliferation diagram of HUVECs cultured in R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel extract for 24, 48 and 72 h. D) The hemolysis experiment before (i) and after centrifuge (ii). E) The hemolysis ratio of hydrogel.

The construction of a versatile red fluorescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA nanocomposite hydrogel. A) Schematic illustration and photographs of the synthesis of red fluorescent R-CDs nanoparticles and R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex (i), derivatives of polysaccharides (CEC, OSA) (ii, iii), and doping R-CDs@α-CD in dynamic polymer networks crosslinked by dual reversible bonds (i.e., imine bond and hydrogen bond) under physiological conditions (iv). B) Schematic illustration and photographs of multiple functions of the novel nanocomposite hydrogel (i.e., red fluorescence, injectability, self-healing, shape-adaptability, self-recovery, biocompatibility and biodegradability).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red fluorescent hydrogels possessing injectable and self-healing properties have widespread potential in biomedical field. It is still a challenge to achieve a biomacromolecules based dynamic hydrogels simultaneously combining with excellent red fluorescence, good mechanical properties, and biocompatibility. Here we first explore hydrophilic inclusion complex of (R-CDs@α-CD) derived from hydrophobic red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) and α-cyclodextrin (α-CD), and then achieved a red fluorescent and dynamic polysaccharide R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel. The nanocomposite hydrogel can be fabricated through controlled doping of red fluorescent R-CDs@α-CD into dynamic polymer networks, taking reversibly crosslinked N-carboxyethyl chitosan (CEC) and oxidized sodium alginate (OSA) as an example. The versatile red fluorescent hydrogel simultaneously combines the features of injection, biocompatibility, and augmented mechanical properties and self-healing behavior, especially in rapid self-recovery even after integration. The R-CDs@α-CD uniformly dispersed into dynamic hydrogel played the role of killing two birds with one stone, that is, endowing red emission of a hydrophilic fluorescent substance, and improving mechanical and self-healing properties as a dynamic nano-crosslinker, via forming hydrogen bonds as reversible crosslinkings. The novel red fluorescent and dynamic hydrogel based on polysaccharides is promising for using as biomaterials in biomedical field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic hydrogels derived from polysaccharides have attracted immense attention owing to the virtues of their excellent properties (e.g., injection, self-healing, biocompatibility and biodegradability) (Deng et al., 2020; Flegeau et al., 2020; Jiang, Diggle, Shackleford, & Connal, 2019; Lueckgen et al., 2019; Mondal & Chatterjee, 2022; Piantanida, Alonci, Bertucci, & De Cola, 2019; Talebian et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019), providing important applications in the biomedical fields, particularly in coupling with minimally invasive treatments (Ayoubi-Joshaghani et al., 2020; Niazi et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Meanwhile, biocompatible photoluminescent hydrogels have specific applications in bioimaging (Mehwish, Dou, Zhao, & Feng, 2019; Wei et al., 2020) and biological detection (Li et al., 2020; Su et al., 2021). Especially, the red fluorescent hydrogel with narrow full width at half maximum benefits from great penetrability, spatial resolution and imaging contrast, as well as minimum autofluorescence and anti-biological background fluorescence interference (He et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Unfortunately, current reports on polysaccharide based dynamic hydrogels are exclusively limited by red photoluminescence, hampering to explore their applications.

At present, the strategies of fabricating photoluminescent hydrogels mainly relied on the conjugating (Lu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021) or doping (Cheng, Hao, & Xing, 2020; Zhong, Zhao, & Liu, 2022) various fluorescent substances, such as organic dye (Mehwish et al., 2019; Wang, Wang, Zhu, Wang, & Xing, 2019; Wang, Xu, Gao, Yao, & Zhang, 2019), lanthanide ions (Li et al., 2019; Li, Young, & Loh, 2019; Li, Zhang, Chen, Gao, & Hu, 2019; Kotova et al., 2022), and carbon dots (Lu et al., 2019; Nayak, Prasad, Mandal, & Das, 2020), into polymer networks. Since most fluorescent hydrogels emit light in blue and green, their applications in the field of biological imaging are limited (Guo et al., 2019; Lu, Si, Le, & Chen, 2022). Because red fluorescent hydrogel has the advantages of anti-biological background fluorescence interference, little damage to cells, and strong penetration (Li, Chen, et al., 2019; Li, Young, & Loh, 2019; Li, Zhang, et al., 2019; Tian, Ma, & Lin, 2019; Yang et al., 2023). It can be combined with near-infrared light imaging technology to make fluorescent hydrogels widely used in biomedical fields such as bioimage and biological detection (Kubota, Kurashina, Zhao, Ando, & Onoe, 2021; Li, Chen, et al., 2019; Mehwish et al., 2019; Qasemi & Ghaemy, 2020). However, many red fluorescent substances have strong intermolecular π-π stacking interaction or dipole-dipole interaction, which seriously quench their fluorescence in the aggregated state (Ipuy et al., 2016; Yuan et al., 2014). In addition, some of them also have biotoxicity, hydrophobicity (Liu et al., 2020; Liu, Kong, & Xiong, 2022) and complicated preparation process (Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017), which limit their practical application performance. So far, a few biocompatible and red fluorescent hydrogels have been reported, but the development of dynamic red fluorescent hydrogels with both injection and self-healing features is still highly anticipated. So far, there is a pressing need in the development of a facile strategy for constructing a dynamic hydrogel with a combination of outstanding red fluorescence, injection, self-healing, good mechanical properties and biocompatibility. Unfortunately, much less effort has been exerted to engineer such versatile hydrogel with inherent red florescence under physiological environments. The primary barrier to explore such hydrogel is how to explore hydrophilic and biocompatible substances with red fluorescence that can be stably incorporated into dynamic polymer networks, and it is still a daunting challenge.

Inspired by the red emissive carbonized polymer dots with narrow full width at half maximum (Liu et al., 2020) and polysaccharide-based dynamic hydrogel (Baei et al., 2021; Heidarian et al., 2020), herein, we report a strategy for fabrication of a versatile red fluorescent and self-recovery nanocomposite hydrogel (denoted as R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA) possessing multiple properties of injectability, self-healing, shape-adaptability, good mechanical properties, biodegradability, and biocompatibility. The novel red emissive nanocomposite hydrogel could be fabricated through doping hydrophilic inclusion complex (R-CDs@α-CD) of red emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) and α-cyclodextrin (α-CD) into dynamic polymer networks, taking dynamically crosslinked N-carboxyethyl chitosan (CEC) and oxidized sodium alginate (OSA) as an example (Scheme 1 ). The key of this strategy is to convert the hydrophobic R-CDs synthesized from ginkgo leaves into hydrophilic substances through the preparation of inclusion complex, so that they can be uniformly dispersed in the hydrogel matrix and inherit photoluminescent properties of R-CDs.

Since the constructed red fluorescent inclusion complex R-CDs@α-CD is hydrophilic, the hydrophobicity problem of R-CDs is overcame, enabling uniform and stable dispersion of the inclusion complex in dynamic matrix, leading to excellent red emissive characteristics of the dynamic hydrogel. Specially, R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex plays the role of killing two birds with one stone, that is, endowing red fluorescence as a hydrophilic photoluminescent substance, and improving mechanical properties and self-healing behavior as a dynamic nano-crosslinker, which interacts with polymer chains of polysaccharides via hydrogen bonds. The synergistic effect of dual dynamic bonds, i.e. imide bonds between CEC and OSA, as well as hydrogen bonds interacted with inclusion complex and the polymer chains, contribute to the dynamic features (injection, self-healing, shape-adaptability, and self-recovery) of the hydrogel. Moreover, the raw materials for synthesizing red fluorescent carbon dots, inclusion complex, and dynamic hydrogel, as well as gelation process are biocompatible, and the overall fabrication is mild. Thus, the photoluminescent and dynamic nanocomposite hydrogel developed by our strategy simultaneously possessing excellent red fluorescence, injection, self-healing, shape-adaptability, self-recovery, and biocompatibility has potential applications as biomedical materials.

Chitosan (deacetylation value 80.9 %, Mw: 50–90 kDa, CAS registry number: 9012-76-4), sodium alginate (M/G = 1:1, Mw: 20,000-32,000, CAS registry number: 9005-38-3) and sodium periodate were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Database Inc. (Shanghai, China), α-Cyclodextrin Hydrate (BR: 98 %, Mw: 990.86, CAS: 51211-51-9) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), Acrylic acid (AR, 58.08 F.W., CAS registry number: 67-064-1) was purchased from Komio Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium(DMEM) bought from Procell Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China), Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), received from CRL-1730, ATCC, LIVE/DEAD cell staining kit purchased from Elabscience, Wuhan, China. All other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (female, 6 weeks old, 180-200 g) were bought from the Experimental Animal Center of Xi’an Jiaotong University, all animals were acclimatised for one week before the experiment. All animal experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Xi’an Jiaotong University Laboratory Animal Center (No. XJTULAC 2020-1230). All animal experiments comply complied with the ARRIVE guidelines.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of R-CDs, polymers, and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The R-CDs were prepared by a solvothermal method in an autoclave containing fresh ginkgo leaf powder and ethanol (130 °C, 5 h) (Fig. S1, Supporting Information S.1). CEC was synthesized by Michael’s reaction (Qu et al., 2019). OSA was synthesized according to reported works (Liang et al., 2020, Wei et al., 2015) (Fig. S2, Supporting Information S.2). The inclusion complex (R-CDs@α-CD) of R-CDs and α-CD was prepared by freeze-drying method. The R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel could be prepared by one-pot method. Firstly, R-CDs@α-CD/CEC solution was obtained by dispersing R-CDs@α-CD (0-4.0 mg/mL) and dissolving CEC in phosphate buffer saline solution (PBS, 0.01 M, pH 7.4). The R-CDs@α-CD/CEC solution was homogeneously mixed with the PBS solution of OSA to form the hydrogel at 37 °C (Supporting Information S.2).

The chemical structure of sodium alginate, OSA, chitosan, CEC were analyzed through 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) (AVANCE NEO 600 MHz, Bruker Corporation, Germany) by dissolving in D2O. The UV–Vis absorption of R-CDs and R-CDs@α-CD was examined by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (T3202S, Shanghai Yoke Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR, Vertex70, Bruker Corporation, Germany) was used to analyze the structure of sodium alginate, OSA, chitosan, CEC, R-CDs, R-CDs@α-CD, α-CD, R-CDs/α-CD (physical mixture), CEC-l-OSA hydrogel, and R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel (Fig. S3, S4). Photoluminescent emission spectra were recorded on an FS5 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). R-CDs and R-CDs@α-CD were dispersed in ethanol and deionized water to obtain 50 μg/mL solution, respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (Discovery TGA 55 analyzer, Anton Paar, Austria) was used to determine the degree of R-CDs, α-CD, R-CDs@α-CD, and R-CDs/α-CD (physical mixture) thermal decomposition and component content. The micromorphology of the freeze-dried hydrogels was examined by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300, Carl Zeiss AG, Germany). The micromorphology of the R-CDs was examined by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOLJEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The pore diameters of the hydrogels were measured by ImageJ software (NIH, MD) (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gelation time, injectability, self-healing, and shape-adaptability
ce:section.ce:para: The gelation time was determined by tube inversion method. Mixing the solution of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC and OSA uniformly in a 5 mL glass vial and placed at 37 °C. Recording gelation time when the tube was inverted and the solution stopped flowing. To test injectability and self-healing, the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel in situ gelation in a syringe was injected into the molds of ginkgo leaf shape and letters through 25 G needle and self-healed at 37 °C (Supporting Information S.4).

The universal materials testing machine (CMT Shenzhen Sansi, Shenzhen, China) was used to examine compression properties of hydrogels. The hydrogel samples were prepared in cylindrical shape with a diameter of 15 mm and a height of 10 mm. The compression test speed was 10 mm/min at 25 °C. The rheometer (DHR-1, TA Instruments, American) was employed to test rheological property of the hydrogels (discs with a diameter of 25.0 mm and a thickness of 1.0 mm) at 37 °C. The experiments of oscillatory mode-frequency scanning, oscillatory mode-amplitude scanning, and oscillatory mode-time scanning tested storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G′′), critical strain point, and self-healing behavior of hydrogels (Supporting Information S.5).

The cytotoxicity tests were conducted using an indirect contact method (Zha et al., 2023) following the manufacturers' instructions of Live/Dead cell staining kit and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8, MedChemExpress, New Jersey, America). The hydrogel extract was prepared according to the ratio of hydrogel to culture medium of 100 mg/mL. Inverted fluorescence microscope (EVOS MS5000 imaging system, Thermo Fisher Scientific, American) was used to observe cell morphology. Hemolytic property of the hydrogel was investigated according to previous research (Zhou et al., 2011). Firstly, taking fresh blood from the heart of four-week-old Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats. Then anticoagulated rat blood was prepared by mixing fresh rat blood and 3.8% sodium citrate solution in a volume ratio of 9:1. The animal experiments were approved by the ethics committee of Xi’an Jiaotong University Laboratory Animal Center (NO. XJTULAC 2020-1230), and complied with the ARRIVE guidelines (Supporting Information).

All quantitative analysis and experiment were carried out in triplicate unless otherwise indicated. Error bars represent the mean ± standard deviation (SD) from at least five independent measurements. Graphs were plotted using Origin 9.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis strategy of red emissive R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis strategy was inspired by red emissive fluorescence and narrow full width at half maximum of R-CDs prepared from fresh Ginkgo biloba leaves (Scheme 1A(i)), as well as dynamic interaction between the derivatives of polysaccharides dangling matched functional groups to form reversible polymer networks (Scheme 1A(ii)-(iv)). The R-CDs could be synthesized by solvothermal method using ethanol extract from fresh Gingko biloba leaves as raw material. Specially, hydrophilic R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex can play the role of killing two birds with one stone in the nanocomposite hydrogel, that is, as the source of red fluorescence and reversible nano-crosslinkers improving dynamic and mechanical properties. The reasonable design enabled the hydrogel to simultaneously achieve multiple functions, including red fluorescence, injectability, self-healing, shape-adaptability, self-recovery, and biocompatibility (Scheme 1B).

However, the as-prepared R-CDs composed of carbon cores and surface hydrophobic polymer chains were prone to aggregate in PBS under physiological environments, limiting their uniform and stable dispersion in dynamic hydrogel matrix (Liu et al., 2020) (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). To overcome the limitation, hydrophobic surface of as obtained R-CDs should be transferred to hydrophilicity. Here, we designed the inclusion complex (R-CDs@α-CD) of red fluorescent carbon dots and α-cyclodextrin, in which hydrophobic R-CDs were enveloped by α-CD (Scheme 1A(i)). Therefore, the R-CDs@α-CD could be uniformly and stably dispersed in hydrogel matrix due to hydrophilicity of the inclusion complex, and formation of hydrogen bonds with polymer chains.

A photoluminescent nanocomposite hydrogel was obtained by homogeneously mixing R-CDs@a-CD with CEC solution and then dynamically crosslinking with OSA polymer chain. Briefly, OSA with aldehyde groups could be facilely obtained through oxidation of sodium alginate derived from seaweed using NaIO4, and the oxidation degree (45 %) was evaluated by iodometry through determining unconsumed periodate (Scheme 1A(ii), Fig. S3, Supporting Information). Water soluble CEC with amino groups could be synthesized by Michael addition reaction between chitosan derived from the shells of shrimp or crab and acrylic acid, and the degree of substitution (35 %) was determined by 1H NMR spectra (Scheme 1A(iii), Fig. S4, Supporting Information). FTIR and 1H NMR spectra revealed that both CEC and OSA were successfully synthesized (Fig. S3, Supporting Information).

To simulate the physiological environment, the photoluminescent hydrogel was prepared in PBS solution (pH 7.4). The dynamic fluorescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel could be facilely prepared by dispensing R-CDs@α-CD suspension into PBS solution of CEC, and then mixed with PBS solution of OSA. The hydrogen bonds attributes to stabilize the inclusion complex in hydrogel matrix (Scheme 1A(vi)). Noteworthy, doping R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex not only endows the hydrogel with red fluorescence, but also introduces nano-crosslinkers to generate more dynamic hydrogen bonds for further enhancing the mechanical properties and self-healing behavior of the polysaccharides based hydrogel. As a result, loading R-CDs@α-CD in dynamic polymer networks achieves the versatile nanocomposite hydrogel with red fluorescence, injectability, self-healing, shape-adaptability, self-recovery, biodegradability and biocompatibility (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of R-CDs and R-CDs@α-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh Ginkgo biloba leaves were used to synthesize R-CDs by a solvothermal approach, exploring the effects of various reaction conditions (ethanol concentration, temperature, and time) on the photoluminescent properties of R-CDs. The optimized preparation conditions of the R-CDs showed that the good quantity could be obtained in the condition of 90 % ethanol extraction, 130 °C heating temperature, and reaction for 5 h. Then, R-CDs powder could be obtained by purification of crude product via dialysis membrane and freeze-drying (Fig. 1A(i), Fig. S1) (Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019; Zhu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the R-CDs showed that the average size of the monodispersed samples was about 3.4 ± 0.98 nm (Fig. 1A(iii)), graphite lattice (100) distances of 0.22 ± 0.03 nm, and a distribution of lattice spacing ranging from 0.13 to 0.30 nm (inserted image Fig. 1A(iii), Fig. S5, Supporting Information) (Ding et al., 2018; Ru et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2016). The above results reveal the successful synthesis of carbon dots. Due to the use of electromagnetic field in TEM analysis, the polymers coating on R-CDs were prone to degradation at high electromagnetic field, which could lead to breakage of polymer chains decorated on the surface of R-CDs. In addition, the polymer structures coated on R-CDs exhibited low contrast of image. Thus, the TEM images mainly recorded the morphology of carbon cores, but the polymer structures were actually difficult to be obtained. Accordingly, the actual measured carbon nucleus morphology of R-CDs observed by TEM cannot accurately determine their real size. Therefore, we determined the size of R-CDs through dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown the DLS pattern in Fig. S4 (i), the average size of R-CDs suspended in ethanol was 204.68 nm, which was significantly larger than the size analyzed by TEM (3.4 nm). In addition, FTIR spectra showed that R-CDs have a strong band at 3100-2800 cm-1, representing hydrophobic long alkyl chains and aromatic rings (Fig. S4(ii), Liu et al., 2020). The result suggests that the R-CDs are composed of carbon nuclei and hydrophobic polymer chains.

However, the as-prepared R-CDs exhibit hydrophobicity in nature, as a large amount of hydrophobic long alkyl chains covered on the surface of carbon dots, so that they are difficult to be dispersed in aqueous solution (Chen et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2020). We measured the dispersibility of R-CDs (1 mg/mL) in water/ethanol mixtures with different water contents, which enabled fine-tuning of the extent of R-CDs aggregation. The solution of R-CDs in pure ethanol emitted intensely red fluorescence, which was gradually weakened with the increase of water percentage because of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. When the percentage of water was up to 50 vol%, the ACQ effect was obviously observed (Fig. S6) (Liu et al., 2020). Due to low bioavailability derived from the poor hydrophilicity of as-prepared R-CDs, their application in biomedical field is limited.

It is well-known that cyclodextrin is a kind of cyclic oligosaccharide with hydrophobic cavity while hydrophilic outer surface, which can form hydrophilitic inclusion complex dispersing in aqueous solution (Lipkowitz, 1998). Thus, to improve the dispersion of R-CDs in aqueous solution, we fabricated inclusion complexes R-CDs@α-CD by host-guest interaction between R-CDs and α-CD. The inclusion complex could be facilely prepared through mixing R-CDs dispensed in ethanol and α-CD aqueous solution (Fig. 1A(ii)). It had been found that the transparent α-CD aqueous solution became turbid gradually under the circumstance of dropwise adding R-CDs ethanol at the beginning of the mixing process. However, after stirring for 48 h, the turbidity phenomenon gradually disappeared, and the mixture retained transparent again which could remain until 12 h (Fig. S7), because the hydrophobic interior cavity of α-CD could embed R-CDs (Cid-Samamed, Rakmai, Mejuto, Simal-Gandara, & Astray, 2022; Wang, Wang, et al., 2019; Wang, Xu, et al., 2019). Moreover, the R-CDs@α-CD could be dispensed in PBS solution (50 μg/mL), and the solution was clear and transparent. When being excited under UV light (365 nm), the PBS solution of R-CDs@α-CD exhibited a red fluorescence emission (as shown in Fig. 1B). These phenomena confirmed the successful fabrication of the inclusion complex with good dispersibility in aqueous solution.

As expected, the R-CDs@α-CD exhibits similar optical properties with R-CDs. Fig. 1B shows the optical properties of R-CDs@α-CD by analyzing of UV–visble and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopies, respectively, and comparing with R-CDs. The UV–visible spectra of the R-CDs@α-CD distributed in aqueous solutions were close to that of R-CDs dispensed in pure ethanol, displaying absorption bands in UV-visble peaked at 412 nm/670 nm and 413 nm/668 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B(i)). When being excited under UV light (365 nm), the R-CDs@α-CD aqueous solution exhibited a red fluorescence emission (inserted photos in Fig. 1B(i)), with very broad absorption from UV to deep red light region. The fluorescent spectra of R-CDs@α-CD displayed main emission peak intensity at 679 nm, displaying a narrow full width at half maximum (c.a. 22 nm), and an excitation wavelength of 416 nm (Fig. 1B(ii)). Moreover, in the PL emission spectra of R-CDs@α-CD excited by different wavelengths of light, the sample exhibited a sharp peak at 675 nm with a shoulder at 729 nm. Interestingly, the emission wavelength did not change when the excitation wavelength increased from 380 to 680 nm, revealing both R-CDs@α-CD (Fig. 1B(iii)) and R-CDs (Fig. S9, S10, S11) have a single and stable photoluminescent center.

The chemical structures and properties of inclusion complex were characterized in a series of ways and compared with R-CDs, a-CD, and physical mixture of R-CDs and a-CDs (simple physical mixture powder of α-CD and R-CDs). The stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H at 3425 cm-1 were observed in the FTIR spectra of R-CDs (Fig. 1C, Fig. S3(ii), Supporting Information). The vibration peak at 2904 cm-1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H bond. The peaks at 1630-1750 cm-1 corresponded to the tensile vibrations of C=O and C=N, as well as the bending vibrations of N-H. The peak at 1610 cm-1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C=C bonds in benzene ring. The tensile vibrations of C=C, C-N, and C-O-C located at approximately 1460 cm-1, 1330-1424 cm-1, and 1047 cm-1, respectively. The peak at 879 cm-1 corresponded to bending vibration of N-H (Li et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2022). In the FTIR spectrum of α-CD, the vibration peak of hydroxyl group (-OH) and C-H was 3411 cm-1 and 2925 cm-1, respectively. The absorption peak of 1157 cm-1 represented C-O group, and the absorption band appeared at 1031 cm-1 wavelength due to the vibration of C-O-C bond (Ghafelehbashi et al., 2020; van der Veen et al., 2000). In the spectrum of physical mixture, characteristic absorption peaks of R-CDs, such as those at 2852 cm-1 and 1737 cm-1, were still present. However, these peaks were not observed in the R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex (Fig. 1C), indicating that the characteristic vibrations of R-CDs were not detectable when they interacted with α-CD structure (Yuan et al., 2012; Zarandona et al., 2020). The above results indicate the formation of inclusion complex, but there is no interaction between physical mixtures.

It can be seen that, the X-ray diffraction patterns of α-CD showed a series of sharp and strong peaks, indicating its crystalline structure (Fig. 1D). However, the curve of the physical mixture displayed a pattern that closely resembles that of R-CDs, with addition of the characteristic crystalline peak of α-CD. This suggested that the observed diffraction patters were straightforward superimposition of the individual diffraction patterns of R-CDs and α-CD, rather than a new distinct pattern. The XRD pattern of the R-CDs@α-CD showed the disappearance of R-CDs characteristic peaks, and the appearance of new peaks in the diffraction of the R-CDs@α-CD indicates the formation of an inclusion complex existing a new crystalline phase (Kaur, Jindal, & Jindal, 2019; Zhang et al., 2022). These results reveal that the structure of the prepared inclusion complex is different from that of a simple physical mixture, and R-CDs were encapsulated around cyclodextrin, thus leading to a decrease in crystallinity.

The thermal stability of R-CDs@α-CD comparing with R-CDs and α-CD was shown in Fig. 1E. The thermal decomposition processes of the R-CDs under went three stages. The first stage involved the decomposition of groups on the surface of R-CDs polymer chain at approximately 330 °C. At the second stage, the polymer chains of R-CDs fractured at 380 °C. The third stage was the pyrolysis of internal crosslinked polymer chains and polymer networks at 480–600 °C. In addition, α-CD is formed in two stages, namely, the dehydration (30–100 °C) and the decomposition of macrocyclic molecules (305–500 °C). The dehydration stage of the decomposition process of R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex almost disappeared because R-CDs was encapsulated by α-CD molecules. The interaction between R-CDs and α-CD further confirmed that the prepared inclusion complex powder was different from the simple physical mixture (Abarca, Rodríguez, Guarda, Galotto, & Bruna, 2016; Hădărugă, Bandur, David, & Hădărugă, 2018). Furthermore, it can be clearly seen that the inclusion complex appeared red emission under UV light, and it did not undergo aggregation quenching (Fig. S12, Supporting Information). The phenomenon reveals that the R-CDs@α-CD can inherit original photoluminescent nature of R-CDs.

Dynamic fluorescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel can be facilely prepared by by mixing R-CDs@α-CD suspension with the PBS solution of CEC, and then mixed with PBS solution of OSA (Fig. 2A). The polymer networks of nanocomposite hydrogel could be crosslinked via dual dynamic bonds. One is imide bond between the aldehyde groups (-CHO) dangling on OSA polymer chains and amino groups (-NH2) of CEC polymer chains. Another is hydrogen bonds formed between R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex and the biomacromolecules. To obtain an uniform hydrogel, the concentration of CEC and OSA was optimized as equivalent mole rate, and total amount of polysaccharides was kept at 11 wt%. The hydrogels loading with various amounts of inclusion complex were denoted as R-CDs@α-CD(x)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel, in which x (0–4.0 mg/mL) is the loading content of R-CDs@α-CD. The gelation time of fluorescent hydrogel could be adjusted by tuning the content gradients of inclusion complex. The hydrogels were obtained within c.a. 100 s at 37 °C, and gelation time obviously descended with the rise of R-CDs@α-CD content (Fig. 2B).

The gelation time gradually decreased from 96 s, 73 s, 63 s, 42 s, to 33 s with the increase in the loading content of R-CDs@α-CD from 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 to 4.0 mg/mL (Fig. 2B). Increasing the amount of R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex bring more hydrogel bonds between the polymer chains of biomacromolecules and inclusion complex, which results in reducing the gelation time. The hydroxyl groups (-OH) on R-CDs@α-CD formed hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups (-OH), amino groups (−NH2) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) on CEC, as well as the hydroxyl groups (-OH) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) on OSA.Thus, the fluorescent inclusion complex could be stably fixed in hydrogel matrix through a large number of hydrogen bonds. However, when the content reached as high as 4.0 mg/mL, the viscosity of precursor solution dramatically ascended in a short time, resulting in rapid gelation.

FTIR characterizations illustrated the successful synthesis of OSA and CEC (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The stretching vibration occurred at 1732 cm−1, indicated that the hydroxyl group (-OH) on sodium alginate was successfully oxidized by NaIO4 to form a typical aldehyde group (-C=O) (Qu et al., 2019). Two strong absorption peaks appeared in CEC at 1568 cm−1 and 1408 cm−1, which were the asymmetric and symmetric telescopic vibrational absorption peaks of -COONa, respectively. The absorption peak of 1595 cm−1 in chitosan attributed to the amino group (-NH2), whereas, in CEC the asymmetric absorption peak with the carboxylate overlapped into a single peak at 1568 cm−1 with increased intensity. The results show a sign that the amino group underwent carboxyethylation (Liang et al., 2020).

The microstructure of the nanocomposite hydrogel loading various R-CDs@α-CD content could be revealed by SEM. As demonstrated in Fig. 2D, all the hydrogels exhibited an integrity and inter-connective porous microstructure of polymer networks, and it became more and more dense with the rise of R-CDs@α-CD content from 0 to 3.0 mg/mL. The morphological evolution can be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds and physical entanglement in the photoluminescent nanocomposite hydrogels, which could be facilitated due to loading of R-CDs@α-CD. Bare CEC-l-OSA hydrogel without inclusion complex exhibited the loosest microstructure with relatively larger pore sizes c.a. 65–75 μm (Fig. 2D(i)). The R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels had well-proportioned porous structures with the pore sizes of c.a. 55–65 μm, 57.5–62.5 μm and 42.2–47.5 μm diameter when the content of nanoparticles was 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mg/mL, respectively (Fig. 2D(ii)-(iv)). However, the micropores structure started to become inhomogeneous and loose (70–90 μm) when the content of R-CDs@α-CD rose to 4.0 mg/mL (Fig. 2D(v)). When the excessive amount of inclusion complex (4.0 mg/mL) was loaded, the PBS solution containing CEC and R-CDs@α-CD became very thick, leading to non-uniform gelation and an increase in the distribution of pore size of the hydrogel matrix. Moreover, an excessive amount of inclusion complex generates more non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonds) with the polymer chains of polysaccharides, consuming the functional groups of CEC (-NH2) and OSA (-CHO) used to form polymer networks via imide bonds. As a result, the R-CDs@α-CD(4.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel exhibited loose and inhomogeneous microstructure.

The optimal emission wavelength of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel and R-CDs@α-CD was 413 nm (excited by 668 nm) and 735 nm (excited by 675 nm), respectively (Fig. 2E(i), Fig. S13, Supporting Information). The emission wavelength of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel was red shifted by 56 nm compared to the R-CDs@α-CD solution. This significant difference in PL emission wavelength may be attributed to the interactions between R-CDs@α-CD and polymer chains of polysaccharides crosslinked in the hydrogel matrix. The existence of a large amount of electron donor groups in the polymer networks, such as amino groups (-NH2) and amide groups (-CO-NH-). These electron-donating groups create radiative recombination centers that enhance the fluorescence intensity of inclusion complex in the hydrogel system. (Ding, Zhu, & Tian, 2014; Chen et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2020). Moreover, the emission bandwidth of the nanocomposite hydrogel was significantly stronger than that of R-CDs@α-CD (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). This may be due to the hydrogen bonds of R-CDs@α-CD with CEC and OSA, which enhance the diversity of R-CDs. In addition, we found that the fluorescence intensity was related to R-CDs@α-CD content. When the R-CDs@α-CD content was increased from 1.0 to 4.0 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of photoluminescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels increased significantly (Fig. 2E(ii)). Therefore, we can regulate the fluorescence intensity of hydrogels by changing the loading content of inclusion complex. Moreover, the fluorescent hydrogel exhibited stable photoluminescence when soaking the hydrogel in hydrogel in PBS solution. The analysis of fluorescence emission from PBS solutions of soaked R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel did not exhibit fluorescent intensity, demonstrating the nanoparticles were stably loaded into the polymer networks (Fig. S14, Supporting Information).

For biomedical materials, the swelling, degradation, and water retention properties are critical to evaluate the potential of hydrogels for drug delivery, cell release, wound dressing, and other applications. Therefore, the swelling, degradation and water retention properties of hydrogels with different R-CDs@α-CD contents were investigated. The swelling test results of the hydrogel in PBS (pH = 7.4) at room temperature are shown in Fig. S15 (Supporting Information). All hydrogels showed good swelling properties. Moreover, appropriate addition of R-CDs@α-CD can reduce the swelling rate of the hydrogel, which may be because the inclusion compound provides more crosslinking sites for the hydrogel, making the hydrogel highly crosslinked, and forming a dense shell on the surface of the hydrogel to prevent water entering polymer networks. The relatively high crosslinking density of hydrogel possesses smaller pore size and the degree of swelling ratio decrease.

Next, the water retention properties of the hydrogels were determined. The water loss rate of all hydrogels increased rapidly within the initial 12 h, reached a constant at 24 h, and remained 5 % to 15 % water (Fig.S16, Supporting Information). The results show that the hydrogel has a good water evaporation rate. In addition, with the increase of R-CDs@α-CD content, the water loss of the hydrogel first decreased and then increased, which was due to the lower water loss rate with the higher crosslinking density of the hydrogel.

As shown in Fig. S17 (Supporting Information), the mass loss of the hydrogel gradually increased with the extension of degradation time. Among them, the CEC-l-OSA hydrogel exhibited the fastest degradation rate, while the degradation rate slowed down when R-CDs@α-CD was incorporated into the hydrogel. After 20 days of degradation, the mass loss of the hydrogel reached 83.3 %, 76.3 %, 72.46 %, 66.3 %, and 76.6 %, respectively, when loading content of the nanoparticles was 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mg/mL. The degradation curves reveal that the degradation rate of the hydrogels can be regulated by controlling the amount of R-CDs@α-CD. This is due to the fact that crosslinked network increases the spatial site resistance effect of the molecules, thus slowing down the degradation of the hydrogels with the rise of crosslinking degree.

Injectable and self-healing hydrogels coupling with minimally invasive approach have widespread potentials in biomedical application (Ayoubi-Joshaghani et al., 2020; Bertsch, Diba, Mooney, & Leeuwenburgh, 2022). However, the lack of red emissive behavior and poor mechanical performance limits their practical applications. In this work, R-CDs@α-CD was doped into polymer networks of CEC and OSA to simultaneously endow photoluminescent characterization and enhance the mechanical properties of the dynamic hydrogel. Compression and self-recovery tests were used to analyze the effect of R-CDs@α-CD content on the mechanical properties of nanocomposite hydrogels. Firstly, compressive loading-unloading test was conducted to evaluate mechanical properties of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel loading with various amounts of R-CDs@α-CD. When R-CDs@α-CD loading content went up from 0, 1.0, 2.0, to 3.0 mg/mL, hydrogels stress was gradually ascended from 34.05, 38.05, 47.05 to 52.18 kPa, and the corresponding Young's modulus elevated from 1.56 to 2.76, 4.43 to 5.73 kPa, respectively. The nanocomposite hydrogel loading 3.0 mg/mL inclusion complex performed the maximum compressive stress, that it was increased by 1.5 times of the bare CEC-l-OSA hydrogel.

However, when loading content further went up to 4.0 mg/mL, hydrogel stress reduced to 41.50 kPa, and the corresponding modulus descended to 5.06 kPa (Fig. 3A(i), (ii)). These results demonstrate that when loading an appropriate amount of R-CDs@α-CD, increases the compression stress of the hydrogel, which is due to hydrogen bonds of dynamic nano-crosslinkers interacted with polymer chains of polysaccharides. Nevertheless, the mechanical properties began to descend. when loading excessive amount (4.0 mg/mL) of inclusion complex.

The effect of R-CDs@α-CD content on the mechanical properties of nanocomposite hydrogel can be analyzed as follows. The mechanical properties of hydrogel relate to the imide bonds crosslinked CEC and OSA polymer chains, as well as hydrogen bonds interacted with inclusion complex and the polymer chains of polysaccharides. Suitable R-CDs@α-CD content consumes the appropriate amount of –NH2 and –COO– groups dangling on CEC and OSA, respectively, to generate hydrogen bonds and more dynamic interactions, leading to enhanced mechanical properties of the hydrogel. However, an excessive amount of inclusion complex leads to occupy more functional groups and inhibit of crosslinking between biomacromolecular polymer chains (i.e., OSA and CEC). In addition, a large amount of inclusion complex induced the obvious rise of the viscosity of mixture, resulting microstructure with non-uniform and large pore size due to rapid and inhomogeneous gelation. The effect of R-CDs@α-CD content on compression properties is consistent with microstructure evaluation of the nanocomposite hydrogels loading various amount of inclusion complex. The enhanced strength and modulus were derived from more tightly crosslinked microstructures, and vice versa. Accordingly, the excellent mechanical properties occurred at optimum loading content (3.0 mg/mL) of inclusion complex.

To evaluate the dissipative capacity of hydrogels, the energy dissipation representing per unit volume upon deformation was calculated according to the area of hysteresis loops of loading-unloading tests. Successive loading-unloading compression properties of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels loading with 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 4.0 mg/mL inclusion complex at 10%-50% strain for 1 cycle were displayed in Fig.3B and Fig. S18. It is noteworthy that all hydrogels presented recoverability under various compression strain (10%-50%) in succession. Taking R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel as an example, successive loading-unloading compressive tests were analyzed to reveal energy dissipation and self-recovery properties, through exploring the dynamic and reversible load-bearing performance of the hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 3B(i), the dissipation area between the loading-unloading curves increased with stretch strain, representing effective energy dissipation. Table S1 shows the values of compressive stress and energy dissipation of the hydrogels at 10% - 50% strain for 1 cycle. With the strain gradually increased from 10 % to 50 %, the energy dissipation of the hydrogel continuously elevated from 0.048, 0.21, 0.57, 1.92 to 10.63 J/m3 (Fig. 3B(ii)), due to the partial dissociation of reversible imine bonds and hydrogen bonds during the deformation processes.

Fig. 3C and Fig. S19 represent the cyclically compressive property of nanocomposite hydrogels loading with various amount of inclusion complex by conducting 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 cycles under 50% strain, respectively. Taking R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel as an example, the stress-strain curves of the 1st, 10th, 20th, 30th, 40th, and 50th cycle did not seem to change much, after successively testing the hydrogels under the large strain, disclosing the excellent self-recovery property of the dynamic hydrogel (Fig. 3C(i)). Table S2 summarize the data of compressive stress and energy dissipation of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels at 50% strain for various cycles. During compressive cycles, the maximum stress did not change obviously, and energy dissipation dramatically ascended with cyclic number (Fig. 3C(ii)). Movie S1 show that the R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel can endure repeated pressure from finger and return to its original shape. The results reveal that the hydrogel possesses excellent self-recovery and durability under large compressive strain.

Fig. 3C and Fig. S19 represent the cyclically compressive property of nanocomposite hydrogels loading with various amount of inclusion complex by conducting 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 cycles under 50% strain, respectively. Taking R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel as an example, the stress-strain curves of the 1st, 10th, 20th, 30th, 40th, and 50th cycle did not seem to change much, after successively testing the hydrogels under the large strain, disclosing the excellent self-recovery property of the dynamic hydrogel (Fig. 3C(i)). Table S2 summarize the data of compressive stress and energy dissipation of R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels at 50% strain for various cycles. During compressive cycles, the maximum stress did not change obviously, and energy dissipation dramatically ascended with cyclic number (Fig. 3C(ii)). Movie S1 show that the R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel can endure repeated pressure from finger and return to its original shape. The results reveal that the hydrogel possesses excellent self-recovery and durability under large compressive strain.

The effect of R-CDs@α-CD content on the mechanical properties of the nanocomposite hydrogels, including compressive stress and energy dissipation at 50% for 1 cycle and at 50 cycles were summarized in Fig.3D and Fig.3E respectively. When R-CDs@α-CD content increased from 0 to 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 mg/mL, the stress at 50 % for 1 cycle of the hydrogels was elevated from 1.80 to 3.16, 5.08 and 6.56 kPa, respectively. The corresponding energy dissipation of the hydrogels gradually ascended from 2.34 to 4.05, 7.23 and 10.63 J/m3. However, when the loading content of further went up to 4.0 mg/mL, compressive stress reduced to 5.77 kPa, and the corresponding energy dissipation descended to 9.50 J/m3 (Fig. 3D). The compressive stress and corresponding energy dissipation at 50 % for successive 50 cycles of the hydrogels demonstrated the same trend (Fig. 3E,). It is considered that R-CDs@α-CD can provide more binding sites for formation of hydrogen bonds interacted between inclusion complex and polysaccharide polymer chains. Much denser hydrogen bonds could be produced with gradually rising R-CDs@α-CD content, leading to enhance energy dissipation of the hydrogels. However, an excessive amount of R-CDs@α-CD occupied more functional groups dangling on the polymer chains, leading to the weakness of crosslinking between biomacromolecule chains, reducing mechanical properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Injectability, self-healing, and shape-adaptability of hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the restoration of gel morphology, dynamic polymer networks and mechanical properties, the behaviors of injectability, self-healing, and shape-adaptability of the photoluminescent hydrogels were assessed by a series macroscopic and microscopic experiments (Yuan, Shen, & Fan, 2021). Firstly, digital monitoring was used to observe the self-healing behavior of broken gels at a macroscopic level. Moreover, rheological recovery tests were conducted to monitor dynamic recovery of the internal structures of polymer networks at a microscopic level. Taking the advantageous of dynamic performances of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel, the photoluminescent hydrogel in situ gelation in a syringe (Fig. 4A(i), (ii), Movie S2, Supporting Information), could be smoothly squeezed through a needle to form various shapes, such as a Ginkgo biloba leaf patter and capitals ("R" and "H"), because dynamic bonds were readily broken and recovery during injection, and then restoration in the process of self-healing. The phenomena reveal that the hydrogel can be freely remoulded to adapt customized shapes. Interestingly, after self-healing for 15 min at 37 °C without any external intervention, the integrated hydrogel constituted by gel particles maintained their original structure in PBS solution for 2 h even under shaken, proving steady shapes and crosslinked network structures of self-healed hydrogel in aqueous solution (Fig. 4A(iii), (iv), Movie S3, Supporting Information). Noteworthy, the self-healed and integrated hydrogel could be easily lifted and then be folded repeatedly. Movie S4 exhibits the injected and self-healed hydrogel with the shape of leaf could endure repeated fold and returned to its original shape. Moreover, the self-healed and integrated hydrogel still maintained good fluorescence performance (Fig. 4A(v)). Fig. 4B shows the microscopy images recorded during the self-healing process on R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel cut in half. The results demonstrate that the notch on the hydrogel could autonomously self-heal within 30 min without any external interference. These macroscopic self-healing behaviors confirm that the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel has excellent injectable and self-healing properties, as well as shape adaptability.

To further understand the restoration of gel morphology, dynamic polymer networks and mechanical properties, the behaviors of injectability, self-healing, and shape-adaptability of the photoluminescent hydrogels were assessed by a series macroscopic and microscopic experiments (Yuan, Shen, & Fan, 2021). Firstly, digital monitoring was used to observe the self-healing behavior of broken gels at a macroscopic level. Moreover, rheological recovery tests were conducted to monitor dynamic recovery of the internal structures of polymer networks at a microscopic level. Taking the advantageous of dynamic performances of the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel, the photoluminescent hydrogel in situ gelation in a syringe (Fig. 4A(i), (ii), Movie S2, Supporting Information), could be smoothly squeezed through a needle to form various shapes, such as a Ginkgo biloba leaf patter and capitals ("R" and "H"), because dynamic bonds were readily broken and recovery during injection, and then restoration in the process of self-healing. The phenomena reveal that the hydrogel can be freely remoulded to adapt customized shapes. Interestingly, after self-healing for 15 min at 37 °C without any external intervention, the integrated hydrogel constituted by gel particles maintained their original structure in PBS solution for 2 h even under shaken, proving steady shapes and crosslinked network structures of self-healed hydrogel in aqueous solution (Fig. 4A(iii), (iv), Movie S3, Supporting Information). Noteworthy, the self-healed and integrated hydrogel could be easily lifted and then be folded repeatedly. Movie S4 exhibits the injected and self-healed hydrogel with the shape of leaf could endure repeated fold and returned to its original shape. Moreover, the self-healed and integrated hydrogel still maintained good fluorescence performance (Fig. 4A(v)). Fig. 4B shows the microscopy images recorded during the self-healing process on R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel cut in half. The results demonstrate that the notch on the hydrogel could autonomously self-heal within 30 min without any external interference. These macroscopic self-healing behaviors confirm that the R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel has excellent injectable and self-healing properties, as well as shape adaptability.

The hydrogel was subjected to an oscillatory frequency sweep, in order to evaluate the effect of R-CDs@α-CD content on the change of storage modulus (G') and loss module (G'') (Fig. 4C). The results show that when the loading content of R-CDs@α-CD went up from 0 to 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 mg/mL, the G' of the hydrogel was ascended from 605.15 to 680.26, 730.36 and 820.47 Pa, and the corresponding G" gradually elevated from 53.55 to 68.12, 80.59 and 105.36 Pa, respectively.

However, when the loading content further increased to 4.0 mg/mL, the G' reduced to 580.28 Pa, and the G" descended to 50.44 Pa. The trend of G' and G" was the same with compression property, that is, the suitable amount of R-CDs@α-CD can interact with the hydroxyl groups (-OH), amino groups (-NH2) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) on CEC polymer chains, as well as the hydroxyl groups (-OH) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) on OSA polymer chains to generate hydrogen bonds as more dense non-covalent interactions, leading to enhanced mechanical properties of the hydrogel. However, overloaded R-CDs@α-CD reduced mechanical properties. Because excessive inclusion complex consuming more functional groups induces limited crosslinking of OSA and CEC, and increases R-CDs@α-CD would induce increased density of concentrated stress around inclusion complex causing further points of stress transmission and structural failure of the hydrogel.

Rheological recovery tests were used to further evaluate the self-healing behavior of dynamic polymer networks. When increasing strain amplitude from 1 % to 1000 % (10 rad/s), the intersection points between G' and G" curves appeared at the strain of 314.9 % (Fig. 4D). The equation of G' and G" showed the polymer networks of hydrogel was destroyed at the point. The rheological recovery behavior was further analyzed by alternating low (1 %) and high (500 %) shear strains with 150 s intervals (Fig. 4E). When cyclic large strain (500 %) and small strain (1 %) were mutually exchanged, the G' and G" still recovered to their initial values after the large amplitude strain was removed and then fixed at 1 % strain. At large strain (500 %), the G' reduced from 816.27 Pa to 247.12 Pa, and the corresponding G′′ increased from 100.12 Pa to 490.69 Pa. When G' was lower than G", it indicates the collapse of dynamic networks under strong external forces. When strain was changed to a small value (1 %), multiple reversible bonds, including imide bonds and hydrogen bonds, were recovered quickly. The results of rheological recovery test showed that the self-healing efficiency of the hydrogel was >95 %. The synergistic effect of the multiple dynamic bonds contributes to rapidly recovery of the reversible polymer networks of the photoluminescent nanocomposite hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of integrated hydrogels after injection and self-healing
ce:section.ce:para: Minimally invasive approach desires the biomedical hydrogels with injectability and good mechanical properties (Correa et al., 2021). Unfortunately, mechanical performance generally contradicts with dynamic characteristics. Here, we found that the self-healed and integrated hydrogel extruded from a needle constituted by gel particles achieved excellent mechanical properties, especially self-recovery performance. The hydrogel could be injected into a columnar model and then self-healed and remoulded to form an integrated hydrogel, which maintained excellent compression performance and rapid self-recovery under loading-unloading compressive cyclic tests (Fig. 5A). Movie S5 demonstrates that the integrated hydrogel after injection and self-healing can endure repeated pressure from finger and return to its original shape. These phenomenon are rarely mentioned in other works, because conventional polysaccharide based hydrogels are generally fragile. To quantitatively evaluate the mechanical properties of self-healed and integrated photoluminescent hydrogels, a serious of compression analysis was conducted. (Fig. 5B(i)). Notably, the compressive strength gradually enhanced from 20.35 to 24.30, 30.70 and 35.65 kPa, when the content of R-CDs@α-CD was 0, 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 mg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding Young's modulus ascended from 1.38 to 2.06, 2.92 and 5.83 kPa, respectively. However, when R-CDs@α-CD content was further increased to 4.0 mg/mL, the compressive strength of the hydrogel descended to 27.41 kPa, and the corresponding Young' modulus decreased to 2.82 kPa (Fig. 5B(ii)). The phenomenon were similar to the mechanical behaviors of the pristine photoluminescent and dynamic hydrogel loading with various R-CDs@α-CD content. These results demonstrate that loading inclusion complex in moderation could enhance the mechanical properties for both pristine dynamic hydrogel and the integrated hydrogels undergone injection and self-healing. To clearly confirm the dissipative capacity of integrated hydrogels constituted by gel particles, energy dissipation was analyzed through conducting compression tests of integrated R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels loading various amount of inclusion complex. Fig. 5C and Fig. S20 show successive loading-unloading compression properties of the integrated hydrogels loading with 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 4.0 mg/mL inclusion complex at 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50% strain for 1 cycle.

Minimally invasive approach desires the biomedical hydrogels with injectability and good mechanical properties (Correa et al., 2021). Unfortunately, mechanical performance generally contradicts with dynamic characteristics. Here, we found that the self-healed and integrated hydrogel extruded from a needle constituted by gel particles achieved excellent mechanical properties, especially self-recovery performance. The hydrogel could be injected into a columnar model and then self-healed and remoulded to form an integrated hydrogel, which maintained excellent compression performance and rapid self-recovery under loading-unloading compressive cyclic tests (Fig. 5A). Movie S5 demonstrates that the integrated hydrogel after injection and self-healing can endure repeated pressure from finger and return to its original shape. These phenomenon are rarely mentioned in other works, because conventional polysaccharide based hydrogels are generally fragile. To quantitatively evaluate the mechanical properties of self-healed and integrated photoluminescent hydrogels, a serious of compression analysis was conducted. (Fig. 5B(i)). Notably, the compressive strength gradually enhanced from 20.35 to 24.30, 30.70 and 35.65 kPa, when the content of R-CDs@α-CD was 0, 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 mg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding Young's modulus ascended from 1.38 to 2.06, 2.92 and 5.83 kPa, respectively. However, when R-CDs@α-CD content was further increased to 4.0 mg/mL, the compressive strength of the hydrogel descended to 27.41 kPa, and the corresponding Young' modulus decreased to 2.82 kPa (Fig. 5B(ii)). The phenomenon were similar to the mechanical behaviors of the pristine photoluminescent and dynamic hydrogel loading with various R-CDs@α-CD content. These results demonstrate that loading inclusion complex in moderation could enhance the mechanical properties for both pristine dynamic hydrogel and the integrated hydrogels undergone injection and self-healing. To clearly confirm the dissipative capacity of integrated hydrogels constituted by gel particles, energy dissipation was analyzed through conducting compression tests of integrated R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels loading various amount of inclusion complex. Fig. 5C and Fig. S20 show successive loading-unloading compression properties of the integrated hydrogels loading with 0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 and 4.0 mg/mL inclusion complex at 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50% strain for 1 cycle.

Noteworthy, the injected and self-healed hydrogels constituted by gel particles exhibited the similar mechanical behaviors as the original hydrogels. When the strain ascended from 10 % to 50 %, the stress of R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel increased from 0.19 kPa to 4.08 kPa (Fig. 5C(i),), and the energy dissipation significantly enhanced from 0.064 J/m3 to 10.41 J/m3 (Fig. 5C(ii)). These data were summarized in Table S3, Supporting information. The loading-unloading curves at 50 % for 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 cycles exhibited that both stress and energy dissipation area between the loading-unloading curves increased (Fig. 5D(i), Fig. S21, Supporting Information). Table S4 demonstrates compressive stress and energy dissipation of the integrated hydrogel at 50% strain for various cycles. After successive 10–50 cycles of separate testing at 50 % strain, compression stress was kept at approximately 3.90 kPa, and energy dissipation obviously increased from 49.19 J/m3 to 300.10 J/m3 (Fig. 5D (ii)). Furthermore, compression loading-unloading values were used to analyze self-recovery properties of injected and self-healed hydrogels loading with various amounts of R-CDs@α-CD.

As shown in Fig. 5E when the content of R-CDs@α-CD increased from 0 to 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 mg/mL, the compressive stress at 50 % for 1 cycle of the hydrogels was elevated from 1.20 to 1.59, 3.16 and 4.08 kPa, and the energy dissipation of the hydrogel gradually ascended from 2.10 to 2.69, 10.44 and 10.41 J/m3, respectively. However, when the loading content further increased to 4.0 mg/mL, the stress reduced to 3.07 kPa, and the corresponding energy dissipation descended to 5.90 J/m3, respectively. The mechanical properties of injected and self-healed hydrogels come from two sources, that is, revisable bonds and the self-healing property. With the rising of R-CDs@α-CD in a suitable range, the hydrogen bonds formed between the inclusion complex and the polymer chains gradually increased, leading to reinforce the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. However, when R-CDs@α-CD content exceed >4.0 mg/mL, the hydroxyl groups(-OH) dangling on R-CDs@α-CD would occupy more functional groups dangling on polymer chains, resulting in attenuation of imine bonds interacted between OSA and CEC polymer chains. At last, leading to the reduction of mechanical properties.

The self-healing efficiency can be evaluated by comparing the compressive strength of the self-healed R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel to that of the pristine hydrogel. As shown in Fig. 5F, the self-healing efficiency of the integrated hydrogel self-healed for 2 h gradually increased from 53 % to 56 %, 61 % and 66 % with the content of R-CDs@α-CD ascended from 0 to 1.0, 2.0, 3.0 mg/mL. However, when the amount of R-CD@α-CD increased to 4.0 mg/mL, the self-healing efficiency decreased to 60 %. The above results reveal that the dynamic nanocomposite hydrogel can be smoothly squeezed by a needle, and then remoulded into an integral hydrogel that retained excellent cyclically compressive and self-recovery performance. Furthermore, the synergistic effect of multiple dynamic bonds (imine bond and hydrogen bond) in the reversible polymer networks can provide triple dynamic features of the photoluminescent nanocomposite hydrogels, i.e., injection, self-healing and self-recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytocompatibility and haemocompatibility of the photoluminescent hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Cytocompatibility is crucial to hydrogel as a biomaterial (Seliktar, 2012). Firstly, Live/Dead Staining Kit was employed to assess the influence of hydrogel extracts on cell viability. In the Live/Dead staining images of inverted fluorescence microscope of control (routine culture without hydrogel extract), CEC-l-OSA, and R-CDs@α-CD(3.0)/CEC-l-OSA , all live HUVECs cultured by hydrogel extracts exhibited strong green fluorescence (calcein-AM), while the dead cell with red fluorescence (propidium iodide) was almost invisible after 24, 48 and 72 h culture. In addition, the HUVECs cultured with photoluminescent hydrogel extracts exhibited similar cell morphology and densities as that of bare CEC-l-OSA hydrogel and control group (Fig. 6A and Fig. S20, Supporting Information). Moreover, cell proliferation was measured by Cell Counting Kit-8. Taking an example, when cultured with the extracts of photoluminescent hydrogel loading with 0.3 mg/ml R-CDs@α-CD, the cells exhibited a proliferation rate similar with that of positive routine culture group (Fig. 6B). The results reveal that the photoluminescent R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogels did not influence the viability of cells, and the photoluminescent and dynamic hydrogels is a promising candidate as biomaterials.

We evaluate the haemocompatibility of hydrogels because it is one of essential prerequisites for the hydrogels utilized for in vivo biomedical applications (Zhu et al., 2019). Taking fresh blood from the heart of four-week-old SD rats, then anticoagulated rat blood was prepared by mixing fresh rat blood and 3.8% sodium citrate solution in a volume ratio of 9:1. As shown in Fig. 6D, all samples appeared bright red before centrifugation, and after centrifugation the red blood cells (RBC) in both the negative control (NC) (saline) and hydrogel groups gathered at the bottom of the centrifuge tubes, while the solution in the positive control (PC) group (deionized water) remained bright red. The percentage of haemolysis was determined by measuring the OD of the supernatant using an enzyme marker, and the haemolysis rate of all samples less than 2%. The outcomes of these experiments demonstrated the excellent haemocompatibility of hydrogels.

In summary, the versatile nanocomposite hydrogel with red fluorescence and dynamic features was successfully fabricated through doping hydrophilic inclusion complex in dynamic polymer networks. Taking an example, the hydrogel can be achieved by doping R-CDs@α-CD into dynamic polymer networks of CEC and OSA, through Schiff base reaction between the aldehyde groups (-CHO) dangling on OSA polymer chains and amino groups (-NH2) of CEC polymer chains, as well as hydrogen bonds formed between rich hydroxyl groups (-OH) dangling on the surface of R-CDs@α-CD inclusion complex, amino groups (-NH2) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) decorated on CEC, and the hydroxyl groups (-OH) and carboxyl groups (-COOH) dangling on OSA. The R-CDs@α-CD/CEC-l-OSA hydrogel exhibits strong absorption and emission in the red light region, as well as good mechanical properties, cytocompatibility and haemocompatibility. The R-CDs@α-CD distributed into dynamic hydrogel matrix plays the role of killing two birds with one stone, that is, endowing red fluorescence as a hydrophilic fluorescent substance, and reinforcing the mechanical and self-healing properties as a dynamic nano-crosslinker, via providing reversible crosslinkings of hydrogen bonds. Compression and rheological tests demonstrated that the hydrogel loading suitable amount of inclusion complex could achieve rapid self-recovery and exhibited high self-healing efficiency even after injection and self-healing. The synergistically reversible mechanism of the dynamic interactions in the polysaccharide polymer chains and nano-crosslinkings contributes to the dynamic features of the hydrogels. Moreover, the hydrogel has excellent cytocompatibility and haemocompatibility provided by cytotoxicity and hemolysis tests. This study offered a new strategy for design and synthesis of such multiple hydrogel with outstanding functions of excellent red fluorescence, injectability, self-healing, shape-adaptability, self-recovery, biodegradability and biocompatibility, holding promise for potential as biomedical materials.
